body_txt: Tattoos have a long history and have over the millennia been signs of different social classes, from royalty to the rags. In more recent times, they have become associated with criminals, sailors, and criminal sailors (a.k.a. pirates ). In fiction, a tattoo can act as a shorthand for "criminal", and at times it's not that far off . Organized crime from Yakuza to Gangbangers have their own codes, and prisoners tell their life stories in pictures . The concept of criminals having tatts isn't new . This trope dates back to antiquity , with the Greeks and Romans using tattoos to brand people as criminals or slaves. note The Greek word stigma (στίγμα) referred to a permanent mark or tattoo, often inflicted as a punishment to brand someone as deviant, criminal, or enslaved. Roman legionaries and slaves were tattooed for identification and control. On top of that, many Greco-Roman writers stereotyped foreign, tattooed cultures—like the Thracians, Scythians, and later the Picts—as wild, uncivilized, and warlike, reinforcing the idea that body ink was a sign of savagery. Meanwhile, in East Asia, Japan developed a similar attitude, though it didn’t start that way. Early on, some indigenous groups (in the Jōmon period) practiced tattooing for spiritual or aesthetic reasons. But over time—during the Kofun period (c. 300–600 AD)—tattoos took on a darker connotation, becoming a mark of punishment and shame for criminals. This shift wasn’t internal: Chinese cultural influence played a big role. Ancient Chinese views, like those of the Han dynasty, saw tattooed peoples (e.g. the Yue and various southern tribes) as non-Han "barbarians, " as the Romans did with their inked enemies. These perceptions carried over to Japan via diplomacy, writing, and Confucian moral frameworks. By the time of the Tokugawa shogunate, Japan was tattooing criminals as a visible sign of disgrace. Still, during the Edo period, voluntary tattooing—elaborate full-body styles—flourished underground among gamblers, firemen, and laborers, inspired by heroic outlaw stories like Suikoden . These rebels wore their tattoos like armor, turning shame into pride. This outlaw aesthetic was adopted by the Yakuza in the 17th century, cementing the now-iconic image of the tattooed gangster with sprawling irezumi . China, meanwhile, didn’t embrace tattooing either. Although punitive tattooing existed (e.g. ci pei , or "tattoo and exile" ), it was rare and viewed as mutilation of the sacred body—something Confucian values frowned upon. The association between tattoos and organized crime began in the 18th and 19th centuries, as triads in southern China and Hong Kong started tattooing members with stylized motifs like dragons on the left arm and white tigers on the right (左青龙，右白虎) , symbolizing mystical power and group allegiance. In the West, the tattoo = criminal shitck was further hardened in the 19th and early 20th centuries by early criminology. Italian physician and criminologist Cesare Lombroso argued that tattoos were physical markers of "atavism "—a regression to a more primitive, violent state of being. According to him, if you had ink, you were born a criminal (or at least had criminal tendencies). Together with longstanding global associations between ink and deviance, it helped cement the idea—across the West and East—that tattoos were a red flag for criminality. The attitudes towards tattoos have softened during the past decades. In an old movie, a character with a tattoo was either a sailor or a thug. In the 2020s, regular middle-class people get tattoos. Indeed, old pirate and prisoner tattoo designs have been adopted into the general lexicon of regular tattoos, prime example being the spider web, which depending on the bearer might have once meant drug addiction, incarceration, or killing a minority, but is now a fashion accessory. Still, the stigma with the past backgrounds makes tattoos popular with rebel types. Even the Rule-Abiding Rebel — or especially the Rule-Abiding Rebel , since it does not actually require committing illegal acts to look rebellious. There is still extreme stigma related to tattoos in Japan, as they are still closely associated with the Yakuza to this day; because of this, tattooed persons are banned from entering the majority of public onsen (hot spring baths) throughout Japan. A tattoo is still a common visual cue in Japanese media for a character of dubious morals or simply a delinquent . This could be regarded as an Evolving Trope ; as tattoos went into mainstream fashion during The '90s , the gangland crooks took their tattoos up a notch with neck tattoos and face tattoos (see Tattoo as Character Type ). If a group of crooks has a defining tattoo, see Group-Identifying Feature . In many cases taking on the tattoo(s) of a group serves as Proof of Commitment , with the prominence and difficulty of removal emphasizing that commitment: a tattoo on the upper arm is easily hidden and can be removed even by crude (if painful and dangerous) methods; a tattoo across the forehead, less so. May be Embarrassing Tattoo when a crook or rebellious punk rock musician goes straight and moves to Suburbia . In futuristic settings, a Barcode Tattoo may be an Orwellian surveillance and control tactic. In Real Life in the 2020s, tattoos have gone mainstream. Suburban soccer moms and accountants get tattoos, and the college student barista at your Bourgeois Bohemian coffeshop might have a full sleeve tattoo that you'd only have seen in The '80s on a Heavy Metal lead singer or a Badass Biker . Some Real Life tattoos have a horrifying usage. Nazis put identification tattoos on Jewish prisoners in WWII concentration camps during the Holocaust.
